title: Local Dead-End — Switch to Domain / Pivot Vectors
body: When local privesc dead-ends on a domain-joined box, you don't need local SYSTEM — the account's DOMAIN REACH is the privesc. Stop grinding the host; start abusing what the account can already touch in AD, and pivot to internal subnets the host can see.
cmd:
# LOCAL PRIVESC DEAD-ENDED on a domain box: the account's REACH is the path.
# Two questions decide where you go:

# Q1 - WHAT CAN THIS ACCOUNT DO IN THE DOMAIN? (BloodHound, mark owned)
#   collect → "Shortest paths from Owned" - the graph is ground truth.
#   Look for: kerberoastable SPNs, AS-REP (no preauth), ACL edges, and
#   sessions of higher-priv users on reachable boxes.
#   BEYOND: marcus had no local SYSTEM, but daniela was kerberoastable and
#   beccy (DA) had a session on a reachable host - that was the whole path.

# Q2 - WHAT INTERNAL HOSTS CAN THIS BOX SEE? (new subnets = new targets)
ipconfig /all        # second NIC? internal range?
arp -a               # known hosts
route print
#   New internal subnet → set up a pivot (Ligolo) and scan it.
#   BEYOND: CLIENTWK1 saw 172.16.x → pivoted in → INTERNALSRV1 + DC.

# Then route to the right AD escalation vector →
warn: The mistake is grinding a workstation for local SYSTEM that doesn't exist. On a domain-joined box with no local privesc, switch frames immediately: the account can already reach AD objects and internal hosts. BloodHound + ipconfig tell you where to go.

choices.label: Map what my account can do — AD escalation
Set up a pivot into a new subnet (Ligolo)
Plan the pivot / map the network
Run BloodHound and read the graph
